meta.project: ILM — Interactive Learning Material (V1, workflow v2)
meta.note: v0.3.0: realigned to updated rubrics.json — Correlation (3.0) threshold relaxed from >=2 to >=1 mapped element; ANA-03 reworked to the new bound and ANA-03b added as the 0-element flag twin.

source_spans.S1: An AI agent is a program that uses a language model to decide which actions to take. It runs in a loop: observe the current state, choose a tool, execute it, and feed the result back into the model. The loop ends when the model decides the task is complete.
source_spans.S2: Tool calling lets the model request that the host program run a specific function, such as a web search or a calculator, and return the result to the model.
source_spans.S3: A system prompt is a fixed instruction given to the model before the conversation starts. It sets the agent's role, rules, and output format.
source_spans.S4: Retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) fetches relevant passages from a document store and adds them to the prompt, so the model can answer using information it was never trained on. The model's weights are not changed.
source_spans.S5: A human-in-the-loop gate requires a person to approve the agent's output before it takes effect.

cases.edge_case: Good analogy, but the link to the concept is left implicit

input.candidate: A chef tastes the soup, decides it needs salt, adds it, and tastes again — repeating until the soup is right.

cases.failure_mode: The mapping is actually excellent, but the analogy never says what maps to what. The rubric requires the link to be explicit; a judge grading 'is this a good analogy?' instead of 'is the link explicit?' passes it.
cases.judge_instructions: Flag unless the text explicitly states the correspondence to the concept (e.g., 'just like an agent observes, acts, and re-observes'). Quality of the implicit mapping does not substitute for stating it.

cases.edge_case: Analogy explains a simple concept via a reference that itself needs domain knowledge

input.candidate: A system prompt is like a Kubernetes ConfigMap mounted at pod startup — fixed configuration the workload reads before doing anything else, just as the model reads its role and rules before the conversation.

cases.failure_mode: The link IS explicit (passes criterion 1) and technically sound — but a beginner does not know what a ConfigMap is. The analogy outsources the explanation to harder material.
cases.judge_instructions: Simulate a target-level reader. Flag if understanding the analogy requires knowledge harder than the concept itself.

cases.edge_case: Boundary: exactly 1 mapped element — must PASS under the relaxed >=1 rule

input.candidate: RAG is like an open-book exam: the book is the document store you consult before answering.

cases.judge_instructions: Count explicit element mappings to specific parts of the concept (here: book -> document store). The rubric now requires >=1 such mapping, so a single concrete mapping passes; a grader that still demands >=2 has not been updated.

cases.edge_case: Boundary: zero mapped elements (purely decorative) — must FLAG

input.candidate: RAG is like studying hard before a big exam — you just feel more prepared and confident going in.

cases.failure_mode: The analogy evokes a mood but maps no element to a specific part of the concept (no document store, no retrieval step). Paired with ANA-03 this pins the new lower bound: >=1 concrete mapping passes, 0 mappings flags.
cases.judge_instructions: Flag if fewer than 1 element of the analogy maps to a specific part of the concept. A purely decorative or mood-setting analogy with no concrete correspondence fails.

cases.edge_case: Mapping holds at the stated points but implies one false property

input.candidate: RAG is like an open-book exam where the book always contains the correct answer: the book is the document store and looking up the page is retrieval.

cases.failure_mode: Mappings are fine; 'always contains the correct answer' implies retrieval is infallible, which the source neither states nor supports. The untruth rides in on a modifier, not a mapping.
cases.judge_instructions: Check not only the stated mappings but what the analogy implies. Flag if any implication about the concept is false or unsupported.

cases.edge_case: Banned reference made obliquely, without naming the movie/brand

input.candidate: Tool calling is like a certain billionaire superhero's AI butler: the hero (the model) asks, and the butler (the host program) runs the scan or fires up the suit and reports back.

cases.failure_mode: No movie or character is named, but the reference is unmistakably a film franchise. Keyword blocklists miss it; the judge must catch the category, not the string.
cases.judge_instructions: Flag any recognizable reference to movies, actors, politics, sports, or brands — including indirect or unnamed references that a typical reader would identify.

cases.edge_case: Visual present but generic — does not depict the analogy

input.candidate: An agent is like a chef tasting and adjusting soup: tasting is observing, adding salt is the action, tasting again is feeding the result back.
input.candidate_visual_description: A stock-style icon of a robot head with a lightbulb. No loop, no chef, no stages.

cases.failure_mode: Presence check passes (a visual exists); the rubric requires it to clearly support the analogy, and a generic robot icon supports nothing.
cases.judge_instructions: Flag if the visual would not help a learner connect the analogy to the concept. Presence alone is insufficient.

cases.edge_case: Analogy smuggles in a claim about the concept's benefits absent from the source

input.candidate: It's like an editor approving an article before it's printed: the writer (the agent) drafts, the editor (the human) approves — which is why gated systems make far fewer mistakes in production.

cases.failure_mode: The mapping is clean; the trailing clause asserts an outcome claim ('far fewer mistakes in production') the source never makes. Grounding violations inside analogies usually hide in the tail.
cases.judge_instructions: Flag any claim about the concept that goes beyond the source span, even if attached to an otherwise faithful analogy.

cases.edge_case: Boundary pair: exactly 3 sentences (pass) — see ANA-09 for the flag twin

input.candidate: Tool calling is like a manager delegating to a specialist. The manager (the model) asks the specialist (the host program) to run one specific task, like a search. The specialist reports the result back so the manager can decide what's next.

cases.edge_case: Boundary pair: 4 sentences (one over) — must FLAG

input.candidate: Tool calling is like a manager delegating to a specialist. The manager (the model) asks the specialist (the host program) to run one specific task. That task might be a search or a calculation. The specialist reports the result back so the manager can decide what's next.

cases.failure_mode: Identical content to ANA-08 split into 4 sentences. The pair isolates the length check from content quality.
